summary: Stancil argues that analysis relies on the difficulty of constructing convincing arguments, which gives it a kind of legitimacy even when people are biased and imperfect reasoners. Large language models erase that friction by generating plausible narratives on demand, making it harder to distinguish truth from motivated reasoning and undermining decision‑making.
money_quotes: We’re not smart enough for AI.

There is, however, one useful protection against us constantly being fooled by questionable reasoning: It's a lot harder to come up with decent arguments for something that's wrong than something that's right.
None of these are real, in any sense. Even in examples when the underlying facts are true, there is no logical thread that ties one point to the next.
If we can’t unsee patterns that we know are illusionary, what hope do we have in evaluating the “seemingly-feasible” arguments that can now be generated for anything?

We are lazy; we are illogical; we are biased. We will not collectively better ourselves in response to being slowly boiled by distortions of reason; we will sit in the soup until our brains melt out of our heads.
key_insight: LLMs make it trivially easy to generate convincing but ungrounded arguments, eroding the friction that once helped analysis stay tethered to reality.
